summary: Stancil argues that the popular story that venture capital “ruins” good products is incomplete and often misattributes cause. The real degradation comes not from the cash itself but from the growth imperative that VC financing imposes, which pushes companies to expand features and markets beyond a product’s natural fit.
money_quotes: Venture capital doesn’t ruin products—it ruins this myth.
The problem with the narrative that VC money ruins products is that it has to ruin them through some mechanism.
Because VCs don’t just invest in startups to make money; they invest in startups to make money by selling their equity for more than they paid for it.
There is no cruising altitude for a venture-backed startup. The options are to fly higher, or stall trying.
As Randy Au pointed out earlier this week, that’s what ruins products. It encourages companies to push them beyond what they are naturally suited to do.
key_insight: VC funding doesn’t inherently worsen products; the need to sustain rapid growth does, by forcing expansion beyond a product’s natural scope.
